summary: McKenzie celebrates a particularly good sales day for his Bingo Card Creator software, where he earned more money sleeping on a Friday morning than he would teaching an English lesson. He reflects on the economics of his side business, noting that his monthly AdWords costs are covered and everything else is profit. He contrasts the agility of being a one-person company with the bureaucratic overhead of large corporations, where even a simple customer interaction required HR, legal, and VP-level approval.
key_points: Passive income from software sales can outperform hourly service work like English tutoring, especially when factoring in time investment
Running costs for a micro-ISV can be remarkably low — $90/month AdWords plus $5/month for e-junkie, with hosting prepaid
A one-person company can respond to customers in any language instantly, while large companies require layers of approval for the same action
The 'do a little work once and keep getting paid for it' model of software sales has fundamental economic advantages over trading time for money
money_quotes: This whole "do a little work once and keep getting paid for it for a while" passive income thing certainly has its benefits.

This is one of the lovely things about being a teeny-tiny slip of a company: you can outmaneuver the guys who have sixteen levels of managers to report to before anything as significant as a pleasantry can be exchanged.
I earned rather more than $80 for… sleeping on a Friday morning, and firing off two emails when I got up.
Even great companies, and I am convinced that employer was and is a great company, have their hangups.
Everything I made for the rest of the month would be gravy, since I don't have any capital purchases planned.
key_insight: The fundamental advantage of a one-person software business is that it decouples income from hours worked and eliminates the bureaucratic overhead that prevents larger companies from being responsive.
